Name: spectral_latent_steering_zero_training
Title: Spectral Latent Steering: Zero-Training Test-Time Optimization via Norm-Preserving Subspace Projection
Short Hypothesis: Test-time optimization can be made both efficient and deterministic by restricting updates to a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace identified via single-pass PCA on initial layer activations, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry.
Related Work: Existing test-time optimization methods often suffer from unpredictable latency and high perturbation overhead, relying on opaque heuristic control mechanisms. Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) dynamically allocates numerical precision based on activation geometry but does not address the optimization subspace. Our proposal differs by focusing on a zero-training, latency-aware framework that uses spectral diagnostics to enforce deterministic latency bounds and reduce hidden-state perturbation overhead.
Abstract: Test-time compute scaling has emerged as a powerful paradigm for enhancing language model reasoning without architectural changes or retraining. However, existing methods often suffer from unpredictable wall-clock latency, high hidden-state perturbation overhead, and reliance on opaque heuristic control mechanisms. We propose Spectral Latent Steering (SLS), a zero-training, latency-aware framework that restricts test-time optimization to a low-rank, norm-preserving subspace. By performing a single-pass PCA on initial layer activations, we identify the dominant spectral directions that govern implicit latent refinement. Subsequent test-time updates are projected onto this subspace, ensuring strict computational budgets while preserving the optimization landscape's geometry. We theoretically ground SLS in information-theoretic bounds, showing that low-rank projections capture the majority of gradient-aligned signal required for reasoning improvement. Empirically, we demonstrate that SLS matches or exceeds full-space perturbation baselines on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, while reducing hidden-state perturbation overhead by over 70% and enforcing deterministic latency bounds. Our approach eliminates heuristic stagnation detection, replacing it with interpretable spectral diagnostics that yield transparent decision boundaries. SLS bridges the gap between foundational optimization theory and practical, scalable inference, offering a reproducible, zero-training path to efficient test-time scaling.
Experiments: Conduct single-pass PCA on initial layer activations to identify the dominant spectral directions.
Project test-time updates onto the identified low-rank, norm-preserving subspace.
Evaluate the framework on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks, comparing against full-space perturbation baselines.
Measure hidden-state perturbation overhead and enforce deterministic latency bounds.
Use interpretable spectral diagnostics to replace heuristic stagnation detection.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The effectiveness of low-rank projections may vary across different tasks and datasets.
Single-pass PCA might introduce computational overhead, although it is designed to be minimal.
Deterministic latency bounds might limit the flexibility of the optimization process.
The framework assumes that the initial layer activations are representative of the optimization landscape, which might not always hold.
